revision.text: #redirect[[en:Musubi]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: 神道における観念の一つ
revision.text:
{{神道}}
'''むすひ'''は、[[神道]]における観念で、天地・万物を生成・発展・完成させる霊的な働きのことである。'''産霊'''、'''産巣日'''、'''産日'''、'''産魂'''などの字が宛てられる。
「ムス」は「ウムス（産むす）」の「ウ」が取れたものとされ、自然に発生するといった意味がある。「苔生す」（こけむす）の「生す」も同根である。「ヒ」は[[霊]]または霊的・神秘的な働きのことである。神道においては、万物は「むすひ」の働きによって生じ、発展すると考える。神道において重要な観念の一つであり、その意義は江戸時代以降の[[国学|国学者]]によって論じられた。
「ムスヒ（ムスビ）」を神名に含む神は多数おり、いづれも「むすひ」の働きをする神と考えられる。
[[造化三神]]の中に[[タカミムスビ]]と[[カミムスビ]]の2神がいる。タカミムスビは[[アマテラス]]が[[天岩戸]]に隠れた時に諸神に命じてアマテラスを帰還させており、カミムスビは殺された[[大国主|オオナムヂ]]を蘇生させている。これらのことから、むすひの神には衰えようとする[[魂]]を奮い立たせる働き（すなわち生命力の象徴）があるとされたことがわかる。
宮中で祀られていた[[宮中八神]]のうち5神にも、神名に「ムスヒ（ムスビ）」が含まれている。うち2神は神産日神（カミムスビ）と高御産日神（タカミムスビ）で、あとは玉積産日神（タマツメムスビ）、生産日神（イクムスビ）、足産日神（タルムスビ）である。玉積産日神は[[古語拾遺]]の「魂留産霊」と同神で、「タマツメ（タマトメ）」は魂を体に留める（[[鎮魂]]）という意味である。生産日神の「イク」は「イキ」（生き、息）と同根で、むすひの働きを賛える語である。足産日神の「タル」は、その働きが満ち溢れている（足りている）様子を示す。
[[カグツチ]]の別名に「ホムスビ」（火産霊）がある。[[イザナミ]]は火の神カグツチを生んだことで陰部を火傷して亡くなった。それを怒った[[イザナギ]]はカグツチを斬り殺すが、その際に多数の神が化生している。多数の神を生み出す神ということで「むすひ」の神なのであるが、ここから「むすひ」の、死んでもなお多くの命を生み出すという、生命の連続性の象徴という意味が見えてくる。「連続」とはすなわち「結び」（むすび）である。同様のことは、日本書紀における[[ワクムスビ]]にも見られる。ワクムスビも死んでから多数の殼物などを生み出している。
「むすび」の3つ目の意味として「掬び」がある。これは「水を掬って飲む」という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は「水を掬ぶとは、人間の体の中へ霊魂を入れ、結合させることである。それを行った人間は非常な威力を発揮する。つまり、水の中へ霊魂を入れ、それを人間の体の中に入れるというのが産霊の技法である」と述べている。すなわちこれは[[禊]]のことである。
[[Category:神道|むすひ]]

'''むすひ'''は、[[神道]]における観念で、天地・万物を生成・発展・完成させる霊的な働きのことである。'''産霊'''、'''産巣日'''、'''産日'''、'''産魂'''などの字が宛てられる。
「ムス」は「ウムス（産むす）」の「ウ」が取れたものとされ、自然に発生するといった意味がある。「苔生す」（こけむす）の「生す」も同根である。「ヒ」は[[霊]]または霊的・神秘的な働きのことである。神道においては、万物は「むすひ」の働きによって生じ、発展すると考える。神道において重要な観念の一つであり、その意義は江戸時代以降の[[国学|国学者]]によって論じられた。
「ムスヒ（ムスビ）」を神名に含む神は多数おり、いずれも「むすひ」の働きをする神と考えられる。
[[造化三神]]の中に[[タカミムスビ]]と[[カミムスビ]]の2神がいる。タカミムスビは[[アマテラス]]が[[天岩戸]]に隠れた時に諸神に命じてアマテラスを帰還させており、カミムスビは殺された[[大国主|オオナムヂ]]を蘇生させている。これらのことから、むすひの神には衰えようとする[[魂]]を奮い立たせる働き（すなわち生命力の象徴）があるとされたことがわかる。
宮中で祀られていた[[宮中八神]]のうち5神にも、神名に「ムスヒ（ムスビ）」が含まれている。うち2神は神産日神（カミムスビ）と高御産日神（タカミムスビ）で、あとは玉積産日神（タマツメムスビ）、生産日神（イクムスビ）、足産日神（タルムスビ）である。玉積産日神は『[[古語拾遺]]』の「魂留産霊」と同神で、「タマツメ（タマトメ）」は魂を体に留める（[[鎮魂]]）という意味である。生産日神の「イク」は「イキ」（生き、息）と同根で、むすひの働きを賛える語である。足産日神の「タル」は、その働きが満ち溢れている（足りている）様子を示す。
[[カグツチ]]の別名に「ホムスビ」（火産霊）がある。[[イザナミ]]は火の神カグツチを生んだことで陰部を火傷して亡くなった。それを怒った[[イザナギ]]はカグツチを斬り殺すが、その際に多数の神が化生している。多数の神を生み出す神ということで「むすひ」の神なのであるが、ここから「むすひ」の、死んでもなお多くの命を生み出すという、生命の連続性の象徴という意味が見えてくる。「連続」とはすなわち「結び」（むすび）である。同様のことは、『[[日本書紀]]』における[[ワクムスビ]]にも見られる。ワクムスビも死んでから多数の殼物などを生み出している。
「むすび」の3つ目の意味として「掬び」がある。これは「水を掬って飲む」という意味である。[[折口信夫]]は「水を掬ぶとは、人間の体の中へ霊魂を入れ、結合させることである。それを行った人間は非常な威力を発揮する。つまり、水の中へ霊魂を入れ、それを人間の体の中に入れるというのが産霊の技法である」と述べている。すなわちこれは[[禊]]のことである。
{{神道 横}} [[Category:神道|むすひ]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[カグツチ]]の別名に「ホムスビ」（火産霊）がある。[[イザナミ]]は火の神カグツチを生んだことで陰部を火傷して亡くなった。それを怒った[[イザナギ]]はカグツチを斬り殺すが、その際に多数の神が化生している。多数の神を生み出す神ということで「むすひ」の神なのであるが、ここから「むすひ」の、死んでもなお多くの命を生み出すという、生命の連続性の象徴という意味が見えてくる。「連続」とはすなわち「結び」（むすび）である。同様のことは、『[[日本書紀]]』における[[ワクムスビ]]にも見られる。ワクムスビも死んでから多数の殼物などを生み出している。

[[宮中]]で祀られていた[[宮中八神]]のうち5神にも、神名に「むすひ」が含まれている。うち2神は神産日神（かみむすひ-）と高御産日神（たかみむすひ-）で、あとは玉積産日神（たまつめむすひ-）、生産日神（いくむすひ-）、足産日神（たるむすひ-）である。玉積産日神は『[[古語拾遺]]』の「魂留産霊」と同神で、「たまつめ（たまとめ）」は魂を体に留める（[[鎮魂]]）という意味である。生産日神の「いく」は「いき（生き、息）」と同根で、むすひの働きを賛える語である。足産日神の「たる」は、その働きが満ち溢れている（足りている）様子を示す。

== 一覧 == *[[高御産巣日神]] *[[神産巣日神]] *[[津速産霊神]] *[[安牟須比命]] *[[己己都牟須比命]] *[[生産日神]] *[[足産日神]] *[[玉積産日神]] *[[和久産巣日神]] *[[火産霊]]
==関連項目== *[[古事記]]
==脚注== <references/>

{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''むすひ'''は、[[神道]]における観念で、天地・万物を生成・発展・完成させる霊的な働きのことである。'''産霊'''、'''産巣日'''、'''産日'''、'''産魂'''などの字が宛てられる。 ==概説== 「むす（産）」は「うむす（産むす）」の「う」が取れたものとされ、自然に発生するといった意味がある。「苔生す（こけむす）」の「生す」も同根である。「ひ（霊）」は霊的・神秘的な働きのことである。神道においては、万物は「むすひ」の働きによって生じ、発展すると考える。神道において重要な観念の一つであり、その意義は江戸時代以降の[[国学|国学者]]によって論じられた。
「むすひ」を神名に含む神は多数おり、いずれも「むすひ」の働きをする神と考えられる。
[[造化三神]]の中に[[タカミムスビ]]と[[カミムスビ]]の2神がいる。タカミムスヒは[[天照大神|アマテラス]]が[[天岩戸]]に隠れた時に諸神に命じてアマテラスを帰還させており、カミムスヒは殺された[[大国主|オオナムヂ]]を蘇生させている。これらのことから、むすひの神には衰えようとする[[魂]]を奮い立たせる働き（すなわち生命力の象徴）があるとされたことがわかる。
[[宮中]]で祀られていた[[宮中八神]]のうち5神にも、神名に「むすひ」が含まれている。うち2神は神産日神（かみむすひ-）と高御産日神（たかみむすひ-）で、あとは玉積産日神（たまつめむすひ-）、生産日神（いくむすひ-）、足産日神（たるむすひ-）である。玉積産日神は『[[古語拾遺]]』の「魂留産霊」と同神で、「たまつめ（たまとめ）」は魂を体に留める（[[鎮魂]]）という意味である。生産日神の「いく」は「いき（生き、息）」と同根で、むすひの働きを賛える語である。足産日神の「たる」は、その働きが満ち溢れている（足りている）様子を示す。
[[カグツチ]]の別名に「ホムスビ（火産霊）」がある。[[イザナミ]]は火の神カグツチを生んだことで陰部を火傷して亡くなった。それを怒った[[イザナギ]]はカグツチを斬り殺すが、その際に多数の神が化生している。多数の神を生み出す神ということで「むすひ」の神なのであるが、ここから「むすひ」の、死んでもなお多くの命を生み出すという、生命の連続性の象徴という意味が見えてくる。同様のことは、『[[日本書紀]]』における[[ワクムスビ]]にも見られる。ワクムスビも死んでから多数の殻物などを生み出している。

revision.comment: Created page with "{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[{{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[{{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[{{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}}]] scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the [[{{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}]], there are the deities [[{{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ}}]] and [[{{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ}}]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}]] when she hid in the [[{{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸}}]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the [[{{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}}]] enshrined in the [[{{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}]], five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ([[{{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}]]). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for [[{{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}}]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[{{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[{{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[{{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * [[{{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}}]] * [[{{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}}]] * [[{{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}}]] * [[{{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命}}]] * [[{{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}]]
== Related Topics == * [[{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}]]
== References == <references/>
{{神道 横}} [[Category:Shinto|Musubi]] [[Category:New pages]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:むすひ]]

Among the [[{{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}}]] enshrined in the [[{{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}]], five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ([[{{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}]]). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for [[{{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}}]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[{{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}}]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[{{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}}]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[{{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * [[{{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}}]] * [[{{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}}]] * [[{{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}}]] * [[{{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命}}]] * [[{{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}}]] * [[{{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>

The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>

{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>

{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion tier 6]][[Category: of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|カミムスビ" to "|カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi"
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion tier 6]][[Category: of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|1=Takamimusubi}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|1=Kamimusubi}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>
{{神道 横}} [[Category:Shinto|Musubi]] [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Musubi]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[ja:むすひ]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|1=Takamimusubi}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|1=Kamimusubi}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>
{{神道 横}} [[Category:Shinto|Musubi]] [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Musubi]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[ja:むすひ]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.
== Overview == The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} scholars since the Edo period.
Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.
Among the {{ill|Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|1=Takamimusubi}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|1=Kamimusubi}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} when she hid in the {{ill|Heavenly Rock Cave|ja|天岩戸|1=Amano-Iwato|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.
Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.
The alternate name for {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|ja|イザナミ}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|ワクムスビ}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}*.
== List of Deities == * {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} * {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神}} * {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命}} * {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命}} * {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}} * {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神}} * {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神}} * {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神}} * {{ill|Homusubi|ja|火産霊}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}
== References == <references/>
{{神道 横}} [[Category:Shinto|Musubi]] [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:Musubi]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Musubi}}[[Category:11]] <!--Musubi--> [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Musubi]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[ja:むすひ]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Musubi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Musubi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''むすひ'''は、[[神道]]における観念で、天地・万物を生成・発展・完成させる霊的な働きのことである。'''産霊'''、'''産巣日'''、'''産日'''、'''産魂'''などの字が宛てられる。 ==概説== 「むす（産）」は「うむす（産むす）」の「う」が取れたものとされ、自然に発生するといった意味がある。「苔生す（こけむす）」の「生す」も同根である。「ひ（霊）」は霊的・神秘的な働きのことである。神道においては、万物は「むすひ」の働きによって生じ、発展すると考える。神道において重要な観念の一つであり、その意義は江戸時代以降の[[国学|国学者]]によって論じられた。
「むすひ」を神名に含む神は多数おり、いずれも「むすひ」の働きをする神と考えられる。
[[造化三神]]の中に[[タカミムスビ]]と[[カミムスビ]]の2神がいる。タカミムスヒは[[天照大神|アマテラス]]が[[天岩戸]]に隠れた時に諸神に命じてアマテラスを帰還させており、カミムスヒは殺された[[大国主|オオナムヂ]]を蘇生させている。これらのことから、むすひの神には衰えようとする[[魂]]を奮い立たせる働き（すなわち生命力の象徴）があるとされたことがわかる。
[[宮中]]で祀られていた[[宮中八神]]のうち5神にも、神名に「むすひ」が含まれている。うち2神は神産日神（かみむすひ-）と高御産日神（たかみむすひ-）で、あとは玉積産日神（たまつめむすひ-）、[[天活玉命|生産日神]]（いくむすひ-）、足産日神（たるむすひ-）である。玉積産日神は『[[古語拾遺]]』の「魂留産霊」と同神で、「たまつめ（たまとめ）」は魂を体に留める（[[鎮魂]]）という意味である。生産日神の「いく」は「いき（生き、息）」と同根で、むすひの働きを賛える語である。足産日神の「たる」は、その働きが満ち溢れている（足りている）様子を示す。
[[カグツチ]]の別名に「ホムスビ（火産霊）」がある。[[イザナミ]]は火の神カグツチを生んだことで陰部を火傷して亡くなった。それを怒った[[イザナギ]]はカグツチを斬り殺すが、その際に多数の神が化生している。多数の神を生み出す神ということで「むすひ」の神なのであるが、ここから「むすひ」の、死んでもなお多くの命を生み出すという、生命の連続性の象徴という意味が見えてくる。同様のことは、『[[日本書紀]]』における[[ワクムスビ]]にも見られる。ワクムスビも死んでから多数の穀物などを生み出している。
== 一覧 == *[[高御産巣日神]] *[[神産巣日神]] *[[津速産霊神]] *[[安牟須比命]] *[[己己都牟須比命]] *[[天活玉命|生産日神]] *[[足産日神]] *[[玉積産日神]] *[[和久産巣日神]] *[[火産霊]]
==関連項目== *[[古事記]]
==脚注== <references/>
{{神道 横}} [[Category:神道|むすひ]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[むすひ]] to [[Musubi]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2012年9月}} '''むすひ'''は、[[神道]]における観念で、天地・万物を生成・発展・完成させる霊的な働きのことである。'''産霊'''、'''産巣日'''、'''産日'''、'''産魂'''などの字が宛てられる。 ==概説== 「むす（産）」は「うむす（産むす）」の「う」が取れたものとされ、自然に発生するといった意味がある。「苔生す（こけむす）」の「生す」も同根である。「ひ（霊）」は霊的・神秘的な働きのことである。神道においては、万物は「むすひ」の働きによって生じ、発展すると考える。神道において重要な観念の一つであり、その意義は江戸時代以降の[[国学|国学者]]によって論じられた。
「むすひ」を神名に含む神は多数おり、いずれも「むすひ」の働きをする神と考えられる。
[[造化三神]]の中に[[タカミムスビ]]と[[カミムスビ]]の2神がいる。タカミムスヒは[[天照大神|アマテラス]]が[[天岩戸]]に隠れた時に諸神に命じてアマテラスを帰還させており、カミムスヒは殺された[[大国主|オオナムヂ]]を蘇生させている。これらのことから、むすひの神には衰えようとする[[魂]]を奮い立たせる働き（すなわち生命力の象徴）があるとされたことがわかる。
[[宮中]]で祀られていた[[宮中八神]]のうち5神にも、神名に「むすひ」が含まれている。うち2神は神産日神（かみむすひ-）と高御産日神（たかみむすひ-）で、あとは玉積産日神（たまつめむすひ-）、[[天活玉命|生産日神]]（いくむすひ-）、足産日神（たるむすひ-）である。玉積産日神は『[[古語拾遺]]』の「魂留産霊」と同神で、「たまつめ（たまとめ）」は魂を体に留める（[[鎮魂]]）という意味である。生産日神の「いく」は「いき（生き、息）」と同根で、むすひの働きを賛える語である。足産日神の「たる」は、その働きが満ち溢れている（足りている）様子を示す。
[[カグツチ]]の別名に「ホムスビ（火産霊）」がある。[[イザナミ]]は火の神カグツチを生んだことで陰部を火傷して亡くなった。それを怒った[[イザナギ]]はカグツチを斬り殺すが、その際に多数の神が化生している。多数の神を生み出す神ということで「むすひ」の神なのであるが、ここから「むすひ」の、死んでもなお多くの命を生み出すという、生命の連続性の象徴という意味が見えてくる。同様のことは、『[[日本書紀]]』における[[ワクムスビ]]にも見られる。ワクムスビも死んでから多数の穀物などを生み出している。
== 一覧 == *[[高御産巣日神]] *[[神産巣日神]] *[[津速産霊神]] *[[安牟須比命]] *[[己己都牟須比命]] *[[天活玉命|生産日神]] *[[足産日神]] *[[玉積産日神]] *[[和久産巣日神]] *[[火産霊]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Musuhi]][[ja:むすひ]][[pt:Musuhi]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11279555]]
Wikidata name: Musuhi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities [[:en:Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi]] and [[:en:Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] when she hid in the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|qq=|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|qq=|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

== List of Deities ==
* {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumusubi-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amusubi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamatsume-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワクムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Homusubi|ja|火産霊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}

Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11279555]]
Wikidata name: Musuhi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities [[:en:Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi]] and [[:en:Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] when she hid in the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

== List of Deities ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Musuhi]][[ja:むすひ]][[pt:Musuhi]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11279555]]
Wikidata name: Musuhi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities [[:en:Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi]] and [[:en:Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] when she hid in the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

== List of Deities ==
* {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

* {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamatsume-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワクムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Homusubi|ja|火産霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11279555]]
Wikidata name: Musuhi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities [[:en:Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi]] and [[:en:Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] when she hid in the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

== List of Deities ==
* {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}

* {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

* {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワクムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Homusubi|ja|火産霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities [[:en:Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi]] and [[:en:Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] when she hid in the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Musuhi]][[ja:むすひ]][[pt:Musuhi]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Musuhi]][[ja:むすひ]][[pt:Musuhi]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Musuhi]][[ja:むすひ]][[pt:Musuhi]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11279555]]
Wikidata name: Musuhi
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities [[:en:Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi]] and [[:en:Kamimusubi|Kamimusubi]]. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] when she hid in the [[:en:Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], while Kamimusubi revived the slain [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *[[:en:Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shūi]]*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for [[:en:Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]] dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of [[:en:Wakumusubi|Wakumusubi]], who generates grains and plants after death in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]*.

== List of Deities ==
* {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamatsume-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワクムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Homusubi|ja|火産霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}

Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} when she hid in the {{ill|Amano-Iwato|en|Amano-Iwato|WD=Q3231835|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|WD=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|en|Wakumusubi|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}*.

{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|en|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|en|Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} when she hid in the {{ill|Amano-Iwato|en|Amano-Iwato|WD=Q3231835|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|WD=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|en|Wakumusubi|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}*.

Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|en|Wakumusubi|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}*.

{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|en|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|en|Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} when she hid in the {{ill|Amano-Iwato|en|Amano-Iwato|WD=Q3231835|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|WD=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|en|Wakumusubi|WD=Q10921330|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}*.

== List of Deities ==
* {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}

* {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワクムスビ}}

{{Referencesの明記|date=2012nd year 9月}}
'''Musubi''' (むすひ) refers to a concept in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} describing the spiritual force responsible for the creation, development, and Completion of heaven, earth, and all living things. It is also written as '''産霊''', '''産巣日''', '''産日''', or '''産魂'''.

== Overview ==
The term "musubi" (産) is derived from "umusu" (産むす), with "umu" (to produce) removed, suggesting natural emergence. The "hi" (霊) represents spiritual or mystical energy. In Shinto, all things are believed to arise and develop through the working of "musubi." It is one of the fundamental ideas in Shinto, and its significance has been discussed by {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} scholars since the Edo period.

Many deities with the word "musubi" in their names are thought to embody this creative force.

Among the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|lt=Kotoamatsukami|lt=Three Deities of Creation|ja|造化三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Deities of Creation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別天津神}}, there are the deities {{ill|Takamimusubi|en|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|1=Takamimusubi}} and {{ill|Kamimusubi|en|Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi|1=Kamimusubi}}. Takamimusubi played a key role in commanding the deities to bring back {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} when she hid in the {{ill|Amano-Iwato|en|Amano-Iwato|WD=Q3231835|lt=Heavenly Rock Cave|1=Amano-Iwato}}, while Kamimusubi revived the slain {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}. These acts demonstrate that the "musubi" deities are associated with invigorating a waning spirit, symbolizing vitality and life force.

Among the {{ill|Eight Imperial Deities|ja|宮中八神|1=Hasshinden|2=ja|3=八神殿|lt=Miyanaka Yatsugami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Imperial Deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} enshrined in the {{ill|Imperial Palace|ja|宮中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Palace|1=Kyūchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453221}}, five include "musubi" in their names: Kamimusubi (神産日神), Takamimusubi (高御産日神), Tamatsume-Musubi (玉積産日神), Ikumusubi (生産日神), and Tarumusubi (足産日神). Tamatsume-Musubi, also known as "Tamodome-Musubi" in the *{{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|WD=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi|1=Kogo Shūi}}*, represents the act of anchoring the soul within the body ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}). Ikumusubi, derived from "iki" (生き, breath), signifies life and vitality. Tarumusubi indicates fullness and abundance in its active force.

The alternate name for {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} is "Homusubi" (火産霊). According to myth, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} dies after giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god, due to severe burns. Enraged, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} kills Kagutsuchi, and many gods are born from this act. Kagutsuchi is thus considered a "musubi" deity, embodying the symbolism of life's continuity and renewal, even in death. Similar themes are observed in the story of {{ill|Wakumusubi|en|Wakumusubi|WD=Q10921330|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|1=Wakumusubi}}, who generates grains and plants after death in the *{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}*.

== List of Deities ==
* {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}
* {{ill|Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja|津速産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumusubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|安牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|己己都牟須比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikitsumusubi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|天活玉命|1=Ame-no-Ikutama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Ikutama|qq=}}
* {{ill|Tarumusubi|ja|足産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Tamatsume-Musubi|ja|玉積産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamatsume-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Wakumusubi|ja|和久産巣日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワクムスビ}}
* {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Homusubi|ja|火産霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}
